items.question: Which is the canonical 'pay memory to save time' move?
items.explanation: Both store results to avoid repeating work.

items.question: Which is the canonical 'pay time to save memory' move?
items.explanation: Both keep only the minimal working state.

items.question: Why is the KV-cache in LLM inference a time-space tradeoff?
options: It pays O(n) memory to drop per-token work from O(n) to O(1)
It pays O(n) memory to drop per-token attention cost from O(n²) to O(n)
It pays O(n) time to save O(n) memory
items.answer: It pays O(n) memory to drop per-token attention cost from O(n²) to O(n)
items.explanation: Cached K/V skip the recompute over previous tokens.

items.question: Why should you bound an `lru_cache` in production?
options: An unbounded cache can grow indefinitely and OOM the process
Bounded caches are required by Python
items.answer: An unbounded cache can grow indefinitely and OOM the process
items.explanation: Unbounded growth is a common cause of memory leaks.

items.question: Which technique reduces edit-distance DP memory from O(m·n) to O(n) with no extra time cost?
items.explanation: Since each row depends only on the previous, you can discard older rows.

items.question: Which axis does quantization (fp16 → int4) primarily save?
options: Memory and often time, at the cost of some quality
items.answer: Memory and often time, at the cost of some quality
items.explanation: Smaller weights fit in less VRAM and can be faster to access, but quality is reduced.
